questions.text: Under what condition an object will have zero displacement but non-zero distance?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In linear motion, an object moves along a straight line. If an object has zero displacement, it means that it ends up at the same position as it started. In this case, the distance travelled by the object will also be zero, as there is no change in position. Therefore, linear motion does not fulfil the condition of having zero displacement but a non-zero distance.

The correct answer is Circulatory motion. Circulatory motion results in zero displacement but non-zero distance because the object returns to its initial position after completing a circular path. Oscillation, on the other hand, does not result in zero displacement because: Oscillation involves back-and-forth motion between two fixed points (e.g., a pendulum or a spring). The object does not return to its initial position after one cycle; instead, it oscillates between two points. Displacement occurs during each cycle, even if the object returns to its starting point after multiple cycles. In oscillation, the distance travelled is non-zero, and the displacement is also non-zero (although it may be periodic). In contrast, circulatory motion involves a complete cycle around a closed path, resulting in zero displacement. So, while both circulatory motion and oscillation involve repeated motion, the key difference lies in the path and the resulting displacement.

The random motion refers to the unpredictable and irregular movement of objects. In this type of motion, an object's displacement and distance can vary widely. While an object can have zero displacement and non-zero distance during random motion, it is not a necessary condition. The object could have any combination of displacement and distance, including both zero and non-zero values.

Oscillation refers to the back-and-forth motion around a central equilibrium point. In oscillatory motion, an object typically has zero displacement when it returns to its equilibrium position. However, the distance travelled by the object will depend on the amplitude of the oscillation. If the amplitude is zero, the object remains at the equilibrium position, resulting in both zero displacement and distance. If the amplitude is non-zero, the object will have a non-zero displacement and distance.

questions.explanation: In circular motion, an object moves along a circular path. If an object completes one full revolution and returns to its starting point, it will have zero displacement since the net change in position is zero. However, the object will have travelled a non-zero distance along the circular path. The distance travelled is equal to the circumference of the circle. Therefore, circular motion satisfies the condition of having zero displacement but a non-zero distance.

The work done by the centripetal force in circular motion is not "reduced" because the centripetal force doesn't result in work in the first place. This is because the centripetal force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion, and work is defined as the product of force and displacement in the direction of the force. When you calculate the work done, you need to consider the component of the force that is in the direction of motion (parallel to the displacement). In the case of the centripetal force, there is no component of the force in the direction of motion, as it always acts perpendicular to the displacement. In mathematical terms, when you calculate work as W = F * d * cos(θ), the θ (angle) between the force and displacement vectors is 90 degrees (perpendicular), and the cosine of 90 degrees is 0. Therefore, the work done by the centripetal force is zero, not half or any other fraction. So, in the context of circular motion, the centripetal force does not perform any work on the object because the force and the direction of motion are always perpendicular.

options.text: The velocity vector is tangent to the circle
options.explanation: The velocity of a particle moving in a circle with constant angular speed is indeed tangent to the circle, as it represents the direction of motion along the circular path.

options.text: The acceleration vector is tangent to the circle
options.explanation: The acceleration of a particle moving in a circle with constant angular speed points towards the centre of the circle, not tangent to it. This centripetal acceleration is essential to keep the particle in its circular path.

options.text: The acceleration vector points to the center of the circle
options.explanation: This statement is true. The acceleration of a particle moving in a circle with constant angular speed always points towards the centre of the circle, providing the necessary centripetal force to keep the particle in its circular motion.

options.text: The velocity and acceleration vectors are perpendicular to each other
options.explanation: The velocity and acceleration vectors for a particle in circular motion are not perpendicular; in fact, they are always at right angles to each other. The velocity is tangent to the circle, while the acceleration points towards the centre.

questions.explanation: When a particle moves in a circle with constant angular speed, its acceleration is directed towards the centre of the circle. This acceleration, known as centripetal acceleration, is necessary to maintain the circular motion. The velocity, on the other hand, is tangent to the circle and perpendicular to the acceleration. Therefore, the correct statement is that the acceleration vector is tangent to the circle, not the velocity.

questions.text: The force which can do no work on the body on which it acts:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the frictional force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. Frictional force opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact. When an object moves on a surface due to an applied force, the frictional force acts opposite to the direction of motion, and it does negative work, opposing the motion. However, if the object does not move or there is no relative motion, the frictional force does no work.

This option suggests that the elastic force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. Elastic force is the force exerted by a stretched or compressed elastic material, such as a spring. When a spring is stretched or compressed, it exerts an elastic force in the opposite direction, trying to return to its equilibrium position. The elastic force can do work when it is involved in stretching or compressing an elastic material, causing a displacement.

This option suggests that the gravitational force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. Gravitational force is the force of attraction between two masses. When an object is at a fixed height (not moving vertically), the gravitational force does no work because there is no vertical displacement. However, when an object moves vertically, such as in free fall or when lifted against gravity, the gravitational force can do work by changing the object's potential energy.

This option suggests that the centripetal force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. The centripetal force is the force that keeps an object moving in a circular path. It always acts perpendicular to the displacement of the object, which means it does no work on the object. The centripetal force only changes the direction of the object's velocity, not its magnitude.

questions.explanation: Option D is the correct answer. The centripetal force can do no work on the body on which it acts, as it keeps the object moving in a circular path by changing the direction of the object's velocity. It does not change the magnitude of the velocity, and it always acts perpendicular to the displacement, resulting in no work being done. The other options are incorrect because they either involve forces that can do work in certain situations (frictional, gravitational forces) or forces that do work when stretching or compressing (elastic force).
